coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
You, Jiajian
Wang, Jiahui
Dong, Xuezhe
Guan, Ruifang
Cao, Duxia
coredata.dc:description: Here, Carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated through a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-aminoethylpiperazine (AEP) and citric acid (CA). The as-prepared CDs have a bright blue emission with a quantum yield of 56%, with good water solubility, light stability and temperature sensitivity. In addition, the photoluminescence of the CDs deriving from two components was experimentally verified: one is amide molecules resulting from the reaction between AEP and CA, and the other is the carbon nucleus coming from the precursors at high temperatures. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is temperature-dependent, and decreasing linearly from 25 °C to 95 °C, with excellent sensitivity and recoverability. A CD/epoxy composite was prepared by doping the CDs into an epoxy resin, which exhibits superior temperature response that may be caused by the greater dielectric constant of epoxy resin. In addition, the fluorescence of the CDs can be efficiently quenched by Hg2+, and the addition of biothiols restore the quenched fluorescence of CDs. The sensor was applied to detect ions in river water and tap water and satisfied results were achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Temperature sensors
Fluorescent composite
Biothiols

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and Cys
Synthesis and structural characterization of the CDs
Temperature, pH and ionic strength effects on the fluorescence emission
Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
Selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs for Hg2+ detection
Mechanism for the detection of biothiols
Specificity of the CD-Hg2+ system for biothiols
Sensitivity of the novel sensor toward Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Typical TEM image of the CDs (inset: the diameter distribution of the CDs), (b) XRD, (c) FTIR and (d) XPS spectra of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. Inset in (a) shows optical photographs of CDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light (365 nm). (b) PL emission spectra of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths.

(a) Cyclic testing of the effect of temperature on the fluorescence emission of the CDs. (b) Temperature dependence of the CD emission. (c) CD/epoxy composites. (d) Temperature dependence of the emission of the CD/epoxy composites.
(a) pH stability and (b) stability in a salt solution of CDs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of an aqueous CD solution (0.01 mg mL−1) in the presence of 100 μM of various metal ions. (b) Selective fluorescence intensity response of the CD solution towards 100 μM Hg2+ (black bars) and 100 μM Hg2+ with interference from 200 μM of other metal ions (red bars) in an aqueous solution at pH 7.4. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in the presence of Hg2+ at different concentrations. (d) Linear relationship between I0/I within the Hg2+ concentration range of 0–45 μM. I0 and I are the emission fluorescence intensities of the CDs at 440 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions. λex = 360 nm. Slit widths: 5 nm/5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Selectivity of the CD-Hg2+ system towards biothiols (green bars) and the interference of 200 μM of other amino acids with 100 μM Cys (purple bars) in an aqueous solution at pH 7.4. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD, CD/Hg2+, CD/Hg2+/Cys, CD/Hg2+/Hcy and CD/Hg2+/GSH mixtures in an aqueous solution. (c) The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs in the presence of Hg2+ (40 μM) upon the addition of different concentrations of Cys within the range of 0–100 μM. (d) The fluorescence recovery factor I0/I versus Cys concentration ranging from 0 to 100 μM. Inset: linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity and Cys concentration. I0 and I are the emission fluorescence intensities of CD-Hg2+ at 440 nm in the absence and presence of Cys, (λex = 360 nm; λem = 440 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram for synthesizing highly fluorescent CD temperature sensors and their application in Hg2+ and biothiol detection.
Performance comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ in tap water and river water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent carbon dots as temperature sensors and “off-on” sensing of Hg2+ and biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Meichen Ecology & Environment Co.,ltd, Zhucheng, Shandong, 262100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Meichen Ecology & Environment Co.,ltd, Zhucheng, Shandong, 262100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, Carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated through a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of N-aminoethylpiperazine (AEP) and citric acid (CA). The as-prepared CDs have a bright blue emission with a quantum yield of 56%, with good water solubility, light stability and temperature sensitivity. In addition, the photoluminescence of the CDs deriving from two components was experimentally verified: one is amide molecules resulting from the reaction between AEP and CA, and the other is the carbon nucleus coming from the precursors at high temperatures. More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is temperature-dependent, and decreasing linearly from 25 °C to 95 °C, with excellent sensitivity and recoverability. A CD/epoxy composite was prepared by doping the CDs into an epoxy resin, which exhibits superior temperature response that may be caused by the greater dielectric constant of epoxy resin. In addition, the fluorescence of the CDs can be efficiently quenched by Hg2+, and the addition of biothiols restore the quenched fluorescence of CDs. The sensor was applied to detect ions in river water and tap water and satisfied results were achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), in a broad sense, refer to carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm in size that contain a large amount of oxygen, hydrogen and other elements, and they have advantages of abundant inexpensive raw materials and simple preparation methods. Compared with other fluorescent substances, CDs have excellent water solubility, high quantum yields, tuneable luminescent spectra, chemical inertness, low toxicity, easy functionalization methods and photobleaching resistance [1–5]. CDs are widely used in various fields, such as the biological field [4], medical displays [6,7], ion detection [3,4,8–12] and photocatalysis [2]. The unique nature of CDs has attracted the interest of researchers and is exceptional in the field of nanomaterials. In view of CDs being prepared by using citric acid and amino group-containing molecules as precursors, efforts are put into exploring their luminescent mechanism and some progress are made. Krysmann and colleagues believe that the luminescence of CDs derives from covalent binding of the precursors to form amide molecules [13]. As the amide molecules gradually form carbon centres, the fluorescence is significantly reduced. Vallan group believe that the high photoluminescence of CDs is due to an accumulation of amide molecular chains under the action of hydrogen bonding and an increased rigidity [14]. In short, there are different conclusions regarding the luminescent mechanism of CDs. More researches are required to make it clear.

Temperature is one of the seven basic physical quantities that can express some type of chemical or physical phenomenon and plays a very important role in human life, research studies and industrial production. Therefore, temperature detection, especially high-precision temperature detection, plays an indispensable role in life, learning, production and research. An optical temperature sensor has the advantages of non-contact sensing, a high heat resistance, a wide temperature range, no influencing the original temperature, and a fast response; People have paid an increasing amount of attention to optical temperature sensors [15,16]. In recent years, fluorescent CDs as temperature sensors has attracted attention of researchers.

Hg2+ can cause environmental pollution. In particular, excessive levels of mercury ions in water endanger animals' and humans' health, and Hg2+ easily accumulates in living organisms and is hardly eliminated [8–11]. The main causes of human mercury poisoning are eating food contaminated by mercury or drinking contaminated water. Therefore, efficient, sensitive and quantitative ways to detect Hg2+ are desirable. Molecular biothiols participate in reversible redox reaction processes and then regulate the homeostasis and metabolism of cells in biochemical pathways. Thus, it plays an important role in the physiological activities of living organisms. Recently, molecular biothiols have received a great amount of attention [6,12,17]. It is well known that Cys deficiency is associated with multiple syndromes, such as the slow growth of children, hair loss, liver damage, skin damage and edema [7]. Hcy deficiency is also associated with Alzheimer's disease [7], osteoporosis [18] and heart disease. The increased risk of vascular disease [19] has a lot to do with Hcy deficiency. The content of GSH is associated with a variety of diseases, such as acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), Alzheimer's disease, alcoholic liver, cardiovascular disease, diabetes and cancer [20–22]. Considering the importance of Hg2+ and biothiols, finding methods to detect them sensitively and selectively is very important.

Here, we describe the production of CDs by a simple one-step hydrothermal treatment with N-aminoethylpiperazine and citric acid (Scheme 1 ). The prepared CDs have a bright blue emission with a quantum yield of 56%. The CDs have temperature-responsive characteristics, their fluorescent intensity decreases linearly with increasing temperature in the range of 25 °C–95 °C, and they have excellent sensitivity and remarkable reversibility/recoverability. CD/epoxy composites were further prepared by uniformly doping CDs into an epoxy resin, which showed significantly enhanced temperature-response. In addition, the fluorescence of the CDs was easily quenched by Hg2+, and then the quenched fluorescence of CD-Hg2+ system was restored by the addition of biothiols. Thus, the prepared CDs has the potential to be used as a sensor for detecting Hg2+ and biothiols.
All materials and characterization methods are described in the supplementary material.

N-aminoethylpiperazine (AEP) was mixed with citric acid (CA) in a molar ratio of 1:1 in 50 μL of water in a Teflon-lined autoclave and then was heated to 190 °C in an oven for 6 h. After the reaction completed, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particle precipitates. Then, the supernatant was dialyzed several times with distilled water and stored at 4 °C until use.

First, 5 μg of the CDs was dissolved in 50 μL of triethylenetetramine (TETA). Then, 350 μL epoxy resin was added to the mixed solution, and the solution was fully mixed by high-speed stirring. Finally, the mixture was placed in a circular mould and cured at room temperature for 1 h to obtain bright-blue luminescent CD/epoxy composites.

Tap water samples were collected from our laboratory, and river water samples were collected from Jiazi Lake in University of Jinan. The collected tap water and river water samples were first centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to remove large particles, and then the small particles were removed by filtration through a 0.45 μm filter. The treated water samples were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence was tested at room temperature by setting the excitation wavelength to 360 nm. First, a CD buffer solution (0.01 mg mL−1) was added to a quartz cuvette, and then a certain amount of ion solution to be tested was added. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after the solution was kept shaking gently by hand and then standing for 2 min. To evaluate the sensitivity of biothiols, a mixed solution of 40 μM Hg2+ and the CDs (0.01 mg mL−1) was prepared at pH 7.4, and the mixed solution was sonicated for 5 min and then used for biothiol detection over a detection range of 0–100 μM. Other amino acid experiments followed a similar procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the fine structures of the CDs, a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to characterize the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the prepared CDs are spherical and have an average diameter of approximately 4.06 nm. The underlying size distribution plot shows CDs’ size range of 2.5 nm–5.5 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1a, inset) shows the obvious lattice fringes with an interspatial distance of 0.34 nm. The visible lattice structure of the macroscopic points indicates that the resulting CDs have graphitic inner cores. To characterize the lattice structure of the CDs, an X-ray diffractometer was also used to characterize the CDs, as shown in Fig. 1b, a relatively wide peak at 23.5°, which is consistent with the layer spacing of the (002) plane of the graphitic structure.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was applied to identify the types of functional groups on the surface of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, peaks appear at 1650 and 1560 cm−1, which are the tensile vibrations belonging to CO and C–N, respectively. The bands at 1320 and 1400 cm−1 are the typical tensile vibration modes attributed to CN and C–N heterocycles, respectively. The peaks at 2950 and 2830 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of C–H. The wide peaks in the range from 3300 to 3500 cm−1 are derived from the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Infrared spectroscopy indicates that a lot of oxygen-containing groups is distributed over the surface of the CDs, such as carboxyl groups, amino groups and amide groups [4]. A plenty of hydrophilic groups on the surface of the synthesized CDs are beneficial for improving the water solubility and stability of the CDs [6,12].

To investigate the elemental composition of the CDs and further confirm the conclusions obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to characterize the CDs in detail. As shown in Fig. 1d, the CDs mainly contain carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. In Fig. S1, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks, and the peak positions are located at 284.43 eV, 286.63 eV and 287.58 eV, which correspond to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N and CO, respectively. The N ls spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 399.43 eV and 401.33 eV, which are respectively assigned to C–N and N–H bonds. The O ls spectrum is divided into two peaks at 531.18.9 eV and 533.18 eV, which belong to the CO and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively. This is consistent with the results obtained from FT-IR spectroscopy analysis, which proves the presence of such groups as –COOH, –OH and –NH on the surface of the CDs.

Based on all the above characterization methods, we believe that the prepared CDs are composed of a graphite-like nanodomain as an inner core (sp2/sp3) with oxygen-containing groups coated on the surface.

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–visible absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation emission spectrum of the CDs. The CDs have a characteristic absorption peak at 347 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* electronic transition of CO [7]. In addition, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CDs are also recorded. It can be seen from Fig. 2a that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs are 347 nm and 440 nm, respectively, indicating that CDs synthesized in this experiment can emit intense blue light. The calculated Stokes shift of the CDs is 83 nm, which is beneficial for distinguishing the target from the background signal in fluorescence bioimaging. Fig. 2b shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength increases from 280 nm to 380 nm, the position of the luminescence peak of the CDs does not change substantially. However, when the excitation wavelength increases to 440 nm, the position of the emission peak significantly redshifts, and the emission peak significantly decreases, which is attributed to a general feature of the formation of CDs. When excited at a wavelength of 340 nm, the CDs show the strongest emission peak with a peak position at 440 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield is 56% (quinine sulfate was used as the reference). We believe that the part emitting strong fluorescence comes from the amide molecule synthesized by the precursor, and the weakly fluorescent part is due to the formation of the carbon core by the amide molecule at high temperature. To prove this point, a small amide molecule, CA-AEP was synthesized, using the same precursors (AEP and CA), as shown in Fig. S2. The fluorescence properties are clearly seen in Fig. S3, and the positions of the absorption peak, the excitation peak, and the emission peak are exactly the same as those of the prepared CDs. More importantly, when the excitation wavelength of the synthesized amide molecule increases from 280 nm to 400 nm, the position of the emission peak remains stable, which is completely consistent with the fluorescent properties of the high fluorescence portion of the CDs. In addition, the infrared and the XPS spectra demonstrate that the CDs are not fully carbonized and contain a large amount of organic functional groups. Therefore, we can conclude that the high-intensity fluorescence of the CDs originates from the amide molecules or the amide molecular chains inside or on the surface of the CDs, and the other luminescent component is derived from the carbon nucleus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature, pH and ionic strength effects on the fluorescence emission
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs fluorescence response for the temperature (25 °C–95 °C) was investigated (Fig. 3 a and b). According to the results shown in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence decreases as the temperature increases. The results show that the CDs exhibit exceptional reversibility and recoverability over the temperature range of 25 °C–95 °C. In other words, the process by which the CDs fluorescence responds to temperature does not cause permanent damage to the surface fluorescent structures of the CDs. There was a good linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and the temperature over the range of 25–95 °C (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 3b), and there was no change in the peak position of the CD emission at 440 nm at different temperatures and 360 nm excitation. As the temperature rises from 25 °C to 95 °C, the PL intensity decreases by approximately 16%. This decrease may be attributed to aggregation of CDs or a synergistic effect between the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrogen bonds, which causes obvious fluorescence quenching [23].

Epoxy resin is a common thermosetting resin and is widely used to package LED chips. Fig. 3c shows optical micrographs of CD/epoxy composite discs of approximately 2 cm in diameter and 8–10 mm in thickness. The cured CD/epoxy composites are transparent, and their fluorescence emission spectra are shown in Fig. 3d. The emission peak of the CD/epoxy composite is blue-shifted by approximately 10 nm compared to that of the CDs’ solution. Notably, the temperature response of the composite is significantly improved. In the temperature range of 25–95 °C, the fluorescence intensity decreases by 35% with increasing temperature, which is more than twice that of the solution state, and the linear results are more stable. The linear equation satisfies I0/I = 0.0074 [°C] + 0.80454 (R2 = 0.99724), where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of the CD/epoxy composite before and after the temperature rise, and the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The blueshift of the emission peak and the enhancement of the temperature response characteristics may be due to changes in the dielectric constant of the environment in which the CDs are located [24,25]. The composite has a wide temperature detection range, and its excellent sensitivity and stability make it suitable for use as a temperature sensor in a variety of environments.

It is well known that the optical properties of CDs are related to their surface state. Changes in the pH or the presence of sodium salt may have an effect on the fluorescent properties of CDs. To verify this, the relationships between differences in the pH and NaCl concentration and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs were investigated. As a result, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increases continuously as the pH increases from 2 to 5. When the pH is increased from 5 to 8, the fluorescence intensity reaches a maximum value and is relatively stable. As the pH further increase, the fluorescence intensity decreases sharply. This phenomenon due to protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs in acidic and basic media. As shown in Fig. 4b, different concentration NaCl solutions (0–1 mol/L) were prepared as solvents for the CDs. As the NaCl concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remains almost unchanged, and the stability of the effective fluorescence is beneficial to the application of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the experimental conditions for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the detection performance of the CD sensor for Hg2+, the pH and incubation time were optimized. The effect of pH on the reaction system is shown in Fig. S4a. As the pH changes from 3.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases. At low pH, most of the functional groups are protonated, and the competition between H+ and Hg2+ decreases the number of available active sites. However, under alkaline conditions, Hg2+ reacts with OH− to form Hg(OH)2, and Hg(OH)2 can easily be converted into a HgO precipitate [26]. Therefore, acidic or alkaline conditions are not conducive to the detection of mercury ions. Since pH 7.4 is similar to the pH of physiological environments, pH 7.4 was selected as the optimal detection condition. To ensure a stable signal, the effect of the incubation time on the reaction system was also tested. Fig. S4b shows that detection takes only 2 min, at which time the fluorescence quenching effect is maximized and then remains relatively stable. Therefore, 2 min was selected as the optimal incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practicality of the CDs, tap water and river water samples containing different concentrations of Hg2+ were measured by fluorescence analysis. The results are shown in Table 2 . The data show good agreement between the concentrations found and the added concentrations. The recovery rates are between 98.8% and 103.1%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the two measurements is less than 1.62%. Therefore, this method can effectively determine Hg2+ in actual water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection of biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that bonds formed between thiol groups and heavy metal ions are much stronger than electrostatic interactions [12,17]. For the CD-Hg2+ system, biothiols will recover the fluorescence of the CD-Hg2+ system (Scheme 1). As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, three biothiols (Cys, Hcy and GSH) restore the fluorescence of the CD-Hg2+ system. The fluorescence enhancement process can reasonably be explained by the fact that, due to the formation of the Hg2+-SR bond, the binding of the biothiol to Hg2+ is stronger than that of Hg2+ to the CDs, which disturbs the interactions between Hg2+ and the CDs. Hg2+ can be easily separated from the surface of the CDs, and thus the fluorescence of the CDs is restored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the CD-Hg2+ system for biothiols
ce:section.ce:para: For the CD-Hg2+ system, pH 7.4 was set, and the incubation time was 3 min (Fig. S5). Cys was chosen as a representative biothiol to evaluate the specificity of the CD-Hg2+ system for the detection of biological mercaptan. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6 that the presence of other amino acids does not obviously interfere with the fluorescence of the CD-Hg2+ system, the addition of Cys lead to fluorescence enhancement, and the fluorescence recovers 91% of the original fluorescence. Similarly to Cys, GSH and Hcy also induce different degrees of fluorescence recovery, but the recovered fluorescence intensity was lower than that of the Cys system. A possible reason for the selectivity of Cys over GSH and Hcy is that Cys has a specific structure that results in preferential coordination with Hg2+. These results indicate that the sensor system has excellent selectivity for biothiols and that the probe is more sensitive to Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the novel sensor toward Cys
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that the linear range for mercury ion detection by the CDs is 0–45 μM, and 45 μM is the end point of the linear relationship when detecting mercury ions. Therefore, to ensure that the CD-Hg2+ system can detect Cys quantitatively, an aqueous solution of the CDs was prepared at the same concentration. After mixing with the 45 μM mercury ion solution, then the CD solution is in a quenched state due to the presence of Hg2+. With the increasing addition of Cys, the fluorescence of the CD-Hg2+ system gradually increases (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence recovery is shown in Fig. 6d. It is clear that the ratio of the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Cys exhibits a linear response to the Cys concentration in the range of 0–40 μM, and the linear equation can be expressed as I0/I - 1 = 0.0177 [Cys] - 0.008 (μM) (correlation coefficient: R2 = 0.99943, n = 9). The limit of detection is calculated to be 0.11 μM, indicating that the CD-Hg2+ system has the potential to be used as a probe for Cys. In addition, the detection limits for Hcy and GSH are 0.11 μM and 0.13 μM, respectively (Fig. S6).

In summary, highly photoluminescent CDs were synthesized and identified, then their luminescence mechanism were initially verified. As a temperature sensor, the CDs had a very wide response range, the fluorescence intensity of CDs linearly decreased from 25 °C to 95 °C, and the CDs had excellent sensitivity and remarkable reversibility/recoverability. CD/epoxy composites were also successfully prepared and exhibited significantly enhanced temperature-responsive characteristics compared to those of the solution-state CDs. More importantly, the prepared carbon dots had relatively high sensitivity and selectivity for the qualitative and quantitative detection of Hg2+ and biothiols, then, satisfying detection results were achieved. This detection method relies on the quenching ability of Hg2+ to the CDs, while the biothiols desorb Hg2+ from CDs’ surface by forming strong Hg2+-S bonds with Hg2+ ions to effectively restored the photoluminescence of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21477047) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM006).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2019.107950.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions

Bioelectrons. 99 (2018) 303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulphur doping: a facile approach to tune the electronic structure and optical properties of graphene quantum dots

Zhang, A recyclable carbon nanoparticle-based fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercapto biomolecules, J. Mater. Chem. B 3 (2015) 127-134.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of polyaniline/carbon dot nanocomposites and their application as a fluorescent probe to detect mercury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, J. Zhang, W. Zou, R. Wang, Facile synthesis of polyaniline/carbon dot nanocomposites and their application as a fluorescent probe to detect mercury, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 41914-41919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence turn-on detection of mercury ions based on the controlled adsorption of a perylene probe onto the gold nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Li, J. Chen, Y. Chen, Y.X. Li, S.A. Shahzad, Y. Wang, M.D. Yang, C. Yu, Fluorescence turn-on detection of mercury ions based on the controlled adsorption of a perylene probe onto the gold nanoparticles, Analyst 141 (2016) 346-351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent probe based on N-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.H. Gao, Z.Z. Lin, X.M. Chen, H.P. Zhong, Z.Y. Huang, A fluorescent probe based on N-doped carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions, Anal. Methods 8 (2016) 2297-2304.

Guo, Heterogeneous synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via anhydrous citric acid and melamine for selective and sensitive turn on-off-on detection of Hg (II), glutathione and its cellular imaging, Sensors Actuators B Chem. 255 (2018) 1130-1138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. J. Krysmann, A. Kelarakis, P. Dallas, E. P. Giannelis, Formation Mechanism of Carbogenic Nanoparticles with Dual Photoluminescence Emission, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 134 (2012 )747- 750.

Maser, Supramolecular-Enhanced Charge Transfer within Entangled Polyamide Chains as the Origin of the Universal Blue Fluorescence of Polymer carbon dots, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 140 (2018) 12862-12869.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Micro-Raman thermometry for measuring the temperature distribution inside the microchannel of a polymerase chain reaction chip

F. Vetrone, R. Naccache, A. Zamarron, A.J. De La Fuente, F. Sanz-Rodriguez, L.M. Maestro, et al., Temperature sensing using fluorescent nanothermometers, ACS Nano 4 (2010) 3254-3258.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hou, F. Zhang, X. Yan, L. Wang, J. Yan, H. Ding, L. Ding, Sensitive detection of biothiols and histidine based on the recovered fluorescence of the carbon quantum dots-Hg(II) system, Anal. Chim. Acta 859 (2015) 72-78.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Guo, Z. Wang, H.W. Shao, X.Y. Jiang, Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions, Carbon 52 (2013) 583-589.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.F. Li, Y.Z. Wu, F.Y. Song, G. Wei, Y.X. Cheng, C.J.Zhu, A highly selective and sensitive polymer-based OFF-ON fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection incorporating salen and perylenyl moieties, J. Mater. Chem. 22 (2012) 478-482.
